justification: Both domain rules (state transition invariant and refund precondition) are present with specific, testable assertions. All three architecture decisions (PATCH endpoint, OrderStateMachine, RefundService) are present with detailed implementation notes — exceeding the minimum of two. Acceptance criteria preserve the error code ORDER_STATE_INVALID verbatim in the first criterion. Terminology alignment is present and addresses the Bird/MJ language gap, mapping domain terms to technical constructs. Task context is one specific, actionable paragraph. No invented information detected — all details trace back to plausible Bird/MJ source summaries. The only minor note is that terminology_alignment doesn't explicitly call out that Bird says 'order status' vs MJ's 'state' field name, but it does reconcile the conceptual gap between domain and technical language, which satisfies the rubric intent.

observations.text: Three architecture decisions provided instead of the minimum two, with RefundService design thoroughly covered
ORDER_STATE_INVALID error code preserved verbatim in acceptance criteria
Testable assertions are concrete and specify exact failure behavior (throw/return domain error, no persistence)
Open questions are thoughtful and flag genuine ambiguities without inventing answers
acceptance_criteria includes a third positive-path criterion (refund succeeds on DELIVERED) which adds completeness without inventing rules

Terminology alignment doesn't explicitly note that Bird uses 'SHIPPED' as a status label while MJ uses 'state' as the technical field name — the reconciliation is implicit rather than called out directly

    must be enforced at the aggregate level — not just at the API layer.
